texts.full_text: in Germany: what are the problems? To determine the role of non-cancer palliative care in inpatient services in Germany, data from the Hospice and Palliative Care Evaluation (HOPE) were analysed. Since 1999, a three-month census has been conducted annually in German palliative care units. Pooled data from 2002-2005 were tested for differences between non-cancer patients (NCs) and cancer patients (Cs). A total of 4182 patients (NC: 3.5%; C: 96.5%) were documented; functional status (using Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group's Performance Status Scale), treatment objectives, medical problems due to disease course and side effects after admission as well as nursing staff perceptions of the patient’s mental state at home situation (as assessed using a seven-level symptom-rating scale) were evaluated. Results indicate differences in median age at death [75 years for non-cancer patients versus 67 years among cancer patients]. At home, an analysis limited to NC with high probability indicates that they had better palliative outcomes as indicated by their significantly lower score on the symptom rating system (13% higher than C). The proportion of NC experiencing hospitalization-related deteriorations in mental state was substantially less compared to C. This suggests some possibility for improving non-cancer care through specialized home-care or a more integrated model that might provide earlier identification, assessment and management tailored to their needs. Also, you don't need .* for prefixes and suffixes - regexp will find a match anywhere in the string, so (.)\1 suffices. This is not an error, just an optimisation. A: You can use a backreference in your regex, but you don't need the + quantifier. You can use the following regex: String input = "wool"; System.out.println(input.matches(".\\1.*")); // true Note that the backreference is \\1, because you need to escape the backslash to make it a literal backslash in the regex. texts.full_text: on supporting me in my journey. What do you think? How can I approach this without hurting them or losing the special connection we have? Is it okay to take things slowly and explain my needs as we go along without pressuring them into anything they're uncomfortable with? This week's question comes from a friend who is relatively new to the LGBT+ scene and is finding it difficult to meet people who share similar interests. They feel like they are constantly navigating through the binary of open relationships and monogamous relationships, but aren't sure if they fit into either category. My advice to them: It's important to remember that there is no one-size-fits-all approach to relationships or identity. We are all individuals with different desires, needs, and boundaries. Just because someone identifies as polyamorous or monogamous doesn't mean that they cannot be friends, or that they cannot have deep emotional connections with others outside of a romantic relationship. The key is to maintain open communication with your partners and to be honest about your desires and boundaries. It's also important to prioritize consent and ensure that everyone involved is on the same page. As for the friend struggling with finding their place in the LGBT+ community, my advice would be to be open about your identity and interests, and to seek out communities that resonate with you. There are so many different types of people within the LGBT+ umbrella, and you might find someone who shares your interests or experiences. Ultimately, it's important to take care of yourself and prioritize your emotional and mental well-being. You don't have to fit into a specific category or mold to be accepted or loved. You just have to be true to yourself and find the people who appreciate you for who you

texts.full_text: to Russia for further enrichment into fuel suitable only for medical reactors in other countries. This would temporarily reduce the amount of material available should Tehran decide to pursue a nuclear weapon rapidly. However, it does not address several critical issues: - Verification and Compliance: The international community has long-standing concerns about Iran's willingness to adhere fully to agreements or inspections by bodies such as the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). There is no guarantee that once this material leaves Iranian soil, any subsequent agreement will be honored. - Time Buying: By engaging in these talks and appearing cooperative on a surface level, Tehran may simply be buying time. This could allow Iran to continue advancing its nuclear program while keeping the diplomatic pressure off for as long as possible. - Remaining Fuel Stockpiles: Even if this deal is implemented fully, it leaves behind enough low-enriched uranium in Iran that can still pose significant risks and concerns about a breakout capability towards producing weapons-grade material. The Obama administration has been cautious but hopeful regarding these negotiations; however, the history of failed diplomatic efforts with the Iranian regime necessitates skepticism. In light of this tentative deal's limitations and uncertainties, it is imperative for Western powers to maintain pressure on Iran through sanctions and international condemnation while preparing a robust strategy that addresses all aspects of its nuclear program. What's going on is that calls What's going on is that calls to `eval` and `quote` in `data.table` need to be properly handled to ensure that the evaluation context is properly set. In the `data.table` package, the environment in which expressions are implemented is important. When you use `eval(quote(a))` within `d[, ...]`, it works correctly because the evaluation context is set to the `data.table` environment, which includes the column `a`. However, when you assign `quote(a)` to `quoted_a` outside the `data.table` context, the evaluation context of `quoted_a` is not the `data.table` environment. texts.full_text: mulls changes to "Gangster Squad" movie after shooting LOS ANGELES The Warner Bros. studio is rethinking its plans for the film "Gangster Squad" in light of a scene featuring a movie-theater shooting, but beyond that Hollywood executives expect little fall-out from the mass killing at a Batman screening on Friday in Aurora, Colorado. Officials at Time Warner Inc-owned Warner Bros. are expected to meet on Monday to discuss whether to remove or edit the "Gangster Squad" shooting scene, or to change the September 7 release date for the film starring Sean Penn and Ryan Goslin as a result of these concerns. The incident in Aurora has prompted many within Hollywood to reassess how violent content is portrayed on screen. While there's no consensus yet about specific changes that should be made, Warner Bros.' consideration reflects an industry-wide sensitivity following the tragedy at one of their own screenings during "Batman" previews over the weekend. Aurora officials have confirmed 12 fatalities and more than two dozen injuries after a gunman opened fire in Theatre 9. This has led to discussions about not just how violence is depicted, but also where these depictions are shown publicly without potential repercussions on audiences' safety or mental well-being during screenings of other films like "Gangster Squad." The film's marketing strategy may need adjustments as executives deliberate over whether maintaining the original release date would be prudent. They must weigh public reaction and possible boycotts against commercial interests. In a broader context, this incident could influence how studios approach future projects involving violence or similar themes in storytelling to prevent potential associations with real-world events that evoke fear among

texts.full_text: distinctive laughing or cackling calls have earned it the nickname "laughing falcon." The bird is specifically adapted for hunting snakes, with a hooked beak designed to tear through tough scales and strong talons capable of seizing struggling serpents mid-flight. Found in open woodland habitats across Central America and South America eastward from Mexico's Pacific coast down into Argentina. Traditionally it has been placed in the subfamily Polyborinae with caracaras, but recent studies suggest closer ties to true falcons or forest falcons depending on classification system used by various ornithological groups. Traditionally it has been placed in the subfamily Polyborinae with the caracaras and forest falcons, but the American Ornithologists' Union's North American Check-list Committee now places it in the same subfamily as the true falcons, while the South American Check-list Committee places it with the forest falcons but not the caracaras, and it has also been considered a subfamily of its own. texts.full_text: Orbiter onto the coordinates provided by the decaying signals around this celestial giant allowed for an unprecedented deep-space navigation technique to pinpoint their exact origin within the tumultuous region. Cygnus-XL, known not only for its intense gravitational pull but also as a landmark in uncharted space territories, served as both a beacon and a challenge. ITs role was pivotal; it acted like a lighthouse guiding explorers through one of the most complex areas of the galaxy where electromagnetic disturbances could easily throw off conventional navigation systems. By using Cygnus-XL’s unique properties to calibrate Orbiter's panels—essentially fine-tuning them to filter out background noise and zero in on subtle variations caused by nearby objects—the team was able to detect faint anomalies consistent with large-scale mining operations or hidden caches. This breakthrough not only brought Belkov closer to reclaiming his valuable feldon crystals but also opened up new possibilities for deep-space exploration, demonstrating the potential of combining advanced technology like Orbiter’s panels with a profound understanding of cosmic phenomena such as black holes. Thus, aligning on Cygnus-XL was more than just setting coordinates; it represented a leap forward in space navigation and resource recovery.

texts.full_text: er at 25, also was an impressive staff officer at that age, said Gordon Rudd, a military historian who teaches at the Marine Corps Command and Staff College. "He was not a Westmoreland, he was not a MacArthur, he was not a high-profile guy. . . . He was more like Omar Bradley, who was a soldier's soldier." In World War II, he fought in 10 campaigns with the 3rd Infantry, in such locales as North Africa and Germany. He received many military awards and decorations, including the Legion of Merit, the Bronze Star, the Purple Heart and the Doughboy Award, the infantry's highest award. Apparently, he did not take the awards too seriously. After the war, while working in Europe under Bernard Montgomery, the British field marshal, Rosson noted that Montgomery was wearing all of his 38 ribbons. According to a story that made the rounds of NATO headquarters, Montgomery then asked each officer in the room, in turn, how many ribbons he had earned. Rosson, who had no idea how many awards he had, answered, "Thirty-nine." Montgomery left the room in a huff, and the American's popularity with British staff officers soared. General Rosson also displayed physical courage in his career. In 1954, while assigned to a military advisory group in Indochina, he flew over the battle of Dien Bien Phu, the bloody altercation that ended French involvement in Vietnam. The first two Americans killed in Vietnam combat, both civilians, died in similar flyovers, downed by withering antiaircraft fire from Vietminh. Gen. Rosson, who subsequently served in Germany and at the Pentagon, returned in 1965 to Vietnam, where he was chief of staff for Westmoreland. He took over I Corps for a Marine commander on leave, a rare case in which an Army officer was in command of a predominantly Marine formation. Gen. Rosson subsequently formed a division of separate units that became the Americal Division and later became deputy commander in Vietnam

texts.full_text: he PayPal API, you can implement a token caching mechanism to avoid the need for a new token for each request. Here's a refined approach to achieve this: 1. Store the Access Token in a Variable: Instead of storing the token in a database, you can store it in a variable in your application. This approach can be more efficient, as it eliminates the need for a database query for each request. 2. Check Token Expiry: Implement a mechanism to check the token's expiration time. If the token is expired, you can refresh it. 3. Refresh the Token: Implement a function to refresh the token. This function can be called when the token is expired. Here's a sample code to illustrate the approach: ```javascript const qs = require('qs'); const axios = require('axios'); const tokenUtils = require('./token-utils'); const baseURL = 'https://api.sandbox.paypal.com'; let token = ''; // Store the token here let tokenExpiry = 0; // Store the token's expiry time here const instance = axios.create({ baseURL }); // Function to get the token async function getToken() { // If the token is not expired, return the token if (tokenExpiry > Date.now()) { return token; } // If the token is expired, get a new token token = await tokenUtils.getNewToken(); tokenExpiry = Date.now() + tokenUtils.getTokenExpiry(); return token; } // Function to refresh the token async function refreshToken() { token = await tokenUtils.getNewToken(); tokenExpiry = Date.now() + tokenUtils.getTokenExpiry(); } // Add the token retrieval and refresh to the request interceptor instance.interceptors.request.use(async (config) => { // Get the token const token = await getToken(); config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`; return config; }, (error) => { return Promise.reject(error); }); instance.interceptors.response.use((response) => { return response; }, async (error) => { // Handle token expiration if (error.response && error.response.status === 401) { // Refresh the token await refreshToken(); // Retry the request return instance.request(error.config); } return Promise.reject(error); }); ``` In the above code: - `getToken` function checks the token's expiry and retrieves the token if it's expired. - `refreshToken` function refreshes the token. - `instance.interceptors.request.use` and `instance.interceptors.response.use` intercept

texts.full_text: T and B lymphocytes in lymph nodes and spleen of patients with Hodgkin's disease. Lymphocyte surface markers of 100 tissue specimens (67 lymph nodes and 33 spleens) of 40 patients with Hodgkin's disease were studied by means of spontaneous E-rosette formation and direct immunofluorescence method. The occurrence of active and total T cells was signigicantly higher (p less than 0.001) in the tumor-involved lymph nodes and spleens, while the frequency of B cells was practically the same in the histologically negative lymph nodes and spsplenic tissues as compared with that in the tumor-involved ones. The ratio of active T cells to total T cells was significantly higher (p less than 0.001) in the tumor-involved lymph nodes and spleens, while no significant difference was observed between histologically negative and tumor-involved tissues regarding this ratio. These findings suggest that there is an accumulation of active T cells in the tumor-involved lymphoid tissues of patients with Hodgkin's disease. This could be due to a stimulation or activation process induced by the presence of the malignant cells, possibly as part of the immune response against the cancerous tissue. The study also highlights the importance of examining both total and active T cell populations in understanding the immunological aspects of Hodgkin's disease. It may provide insights into the mechanisms involved in the immune system's interaction with this type of lymphoma, potentially leading to better diagnostic tools or therapeutic strategies targeting these cells. Further research is needed to explore the functional roles of these T cells and their interactions with other components of the immune system within the tumor microenvironment. This could help elucidate the complex dynamics between cancerous cells and the host's immune response in Hodgkin's disease, contributing to advancements in treatment approaches and patient outcomes. In conclusion, this study underscores the significance of analyzing lymphocyte surface markers in understanding the immunological characteristics of Hodgkin's disease. It highlights the potential for targeted therapies aimed at modulating T cell activity

texts.full_text: accumulation of pathological white blood cells. Currently, chemotherapy is a major pharmacological approach in the treatment of leukemia \[[@CR1]\]. To improve the efficacy of anticancer agents and increase the therapeutic index, many strategies have been designed. One of the approaches for leukemia treatment is the development of BH3 mimetic compounds \[[@CR2], [@cR3]\]. The BH3 mimetics are small molecule antagonists of the anti-apoptotic members of Bcl-2 protein family. These agents mimic the structure and function of BH3-only proteins and interact with anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 proteins at their BH3-binding hydrophobic groove \[[@CR4]\]. Members of the Bcl-2 family are thought to be one of the crucial mediators involved in mitochondrial cell death pathway \[[@CR5]\]. Anti-apoptotic members of Bcl-2 family are frequently overexpressed in almost all types and subtypes of leukemia, indicating the importance of these molecules in disease pathogenesis and treatment failure \[[@CR6]\]. The inhibition of anti-apoptotic proteins of Bcl-2 familiy by BH3 mimetics results in the activation of pro-apoptotic proteins, in turn inducing mitochondrial dysfunction, cytochrome C release, and activation of the caspase cascade, finally resulting in cell death. In this study, we compared the effects of the BH3 mimetics ABT-263 and ABT-737, on apoptosis induction in human leukemia cell lines of different subtypes. The goal of the study was to identify and compare the effects of BH3 mimetics on leukemia cell lines, and to identify differences between cell lines of different subtypes. We also determined the effect of BH3 mimetics on mitochondrial potential, and determined the cytotoxic effects of the BH3 mimetics.
